ir_specs.statement: The pwd command prints the name of the current directory.
ir_specs.source_section: 19.1 pwd: Print working directory
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: pwd prints the name of the current directory.

ir_specs.statement: The basename command removes any leading directory components from a name.
ir_specs.source_section: 18.1 basename: Strip directory and suffix from a file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: basename removes leading directory components from name.

ir_specs.statement: The dirname command prints all but the final slash-delimited component of each name.
ir_specs.source_section: 18.2 dirname: Strip last file name component
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: dirname prints all but the final slash-delimited component.

ir_specs.statement: If the input string contains no slash, dirname prints '.'.
conditions: The input string contains no slash.
ir_specs.source_section: 18.2 dirname: Strip last file name component
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: If the string contains no slash, dirname prints '.' meaning the current directory.

ir_specs.statement: The true command returns an exit status of 0, meaning success.
ir_specs.source_section: 16.2 true: Do nothing, successfully
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: true does nothing except return exit status 0, meaning success.
ir_specs.notes: The manual notes that in most modern shells, true is often a shell built-in.

ir_specs.statement: The false command returns an exit status of 1, meaning failure.
ir_specs.source_section: 16.1 false: Do nothing, unsuccessfully
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: false does nothing except return exit status 1, meaning failure.
ir_specs.notes: The manual notes that in most modern shells, false is often a shell built-in.

ir_specs.statement: With --logical, pwd outputs the contents of PWD if PWD is an absolute name of the current directory with no '.' or '..' components.
ir_specs.source_section: 19.1 pwd: Print working directory
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: pwd --logical uses PWD when it is an absolute current-directory path without dot components.
ir_specs.notes: Behavior depends on the contents of the PWD environment variable.

ir_specs.statement: With --physical, pwd prints a fully resolved name for the current directory with no symbolic links in the printed path.
ir_specs.source_section: 19.1 pwd: Print working directory
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: pwd --physical prints a fully resolved name with no symbolic links in the output path.

ir_specs.statement: If basename is given a suffix identical to the end of the name, that suffix is removed.
ir_specs.source_section: 18.1 basename: Strip directory and suffix from a file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: A suffix identical to the end of the name is removed by basename.

ir_specs.statement: The basename --zero option outputs a zero byte at the end of each line rather than a newline.
ir_specs.source_section: 18.1 basename: Strip directory and suffix from a file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: basename --zero uses an ASCII NUL terminator instead of a newline.

ir_specs.statement: The echo command writes each given string to standard output with a space between strings and a newline after the last one.
ir_specs.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: echo writes each string to standard output with spaces between strings and a trailing newline.
ir_specs.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

ir_specs.statement: The echo -n option does not output the trailing newline.
ir_specs.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: echo -n suppresses the trailing newline.
ir_specs.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

ir_specs.statement: The echo -e option enables interpretation of backslash-escaped characters in each string.
ir_specs.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: echo -e enables backslash escape interpretation.
ir_specs.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

ir_specs.statement: The echo -E option disables interpretation of backslash escapes in each string.
ir_specs.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: echo -E disables backslash escape interpretation and is the default.
ir_specs.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

ir_specs.statement: The printf command does formatted printing of text.
ir_specs.source_section: 15.2 printf: Format and print data
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: printf does formatted printing of text.
ir_specs.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

ir_specs.statement: The printf command prints the format string, interpreting percent directives and backslash escapes to format numeric and string arguments.
ir_specs.source_section: 15.2 printf: Format and print data
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: printf interprets percent directives and backslash escapes in the format string.
ir_specs.notes: Shell built-ins and aliases may behave differently from the standalone program documented here.

ir_specs.statement: The yes command prints its command line arguments, separated by spaces and followed by a newline, forever until it is killed.
ir_specs.source_section: 15.3 yes: Print a string until interrupted
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: yes repeats its arguments, separated by spaces and followed by a newline, until killed.

ir_specs.statement: If yes is invoked with no arguments, it prints 'y' followed by a newline forever until killed.
ir_specs.source_section: 15.3 yes: Print a string until interrupted
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: With no arguments, yes prints y followed by a newline until killed.

ir_specs.statement: The printenv command prints environment variable values.
ir_specs.source_section: 19.3 printenv: Print all or some environment variables
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: printenv prints environment variable values.

ir_specs.statement: If no variables are specified, printenv prints the value of every environment variable.
ir_specs.source_section: 19.3 printenv: Print all or some environment variables
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: When no variable is specified, printenv prints every environment variable value.

ir_specs.statement: The printenv --null option outputs a zero byte at the end of each line rather than a newline.
ir_specs.source_section: 19.3 printenv: Print all or some environment variables
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: printenv --null uses an ASCII NUL terminator instead of a newline.

ir_specs.statement: The tty command prints the file name of the terminal connected to standard input.
ir_specs.source_section: 19.4 tty: Print file name of terminal on standard input
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: tty prints the file name of the terminal connected to standard input.

ir_specs.statement: If standard input is not a terminal, tty prints 'not a tty'.
ir_specs.source_section: 19.4 tty: Print file name of terminal on standard input
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: tty prints not a tty when standard input is not a terminal.

ir_specs.statement: The logname command prints the calling user's name as found in a system-maintained file.
ir_specs.source_section: 20.2 logname: Print current login name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: logname prints the calling user's name from a system-maintained file.
ir_specs.notes: The manual cites files such as /var/run/utmp or /etc/utmp.

ir_specs.statement: If there is no entry for the calling process, logname exits with status 1.
ir_specs.source_section: 20.2 logname: Print current login name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: If there is no entry for the calling process, logname exits with status 1.

ir_specs.statement: The whoami command prints the user name associated with the current effective user ID.
ir_specs.source_section: 20.3 whoami: Print effective user name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: whoami prints the user name associated with the current effective user ID.

ir_specs.statement: The whoami command is equivalent to the command id -un.
ir_specs.source_section: 20.3 whoami: Print effective user name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: whoami is equivalent to id -un.

ir_specs.statement: The date command displays the date and time.
ir_specs.source_section: 21.1 date: Print or set system date and time
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: date displays the date and time.
ir_specs.notes: Setting the system time depends on runtime privileges and environment.

ir_specs.statement: With the --set option, date sets the date and time before displaying it.
ir_specs.source_section: 21.1 date: Print or set system date and time
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: With --set, date sets the date and time before displaying it.
ir_specs.notes: Setting the system time depends on runtime privileges and environment.

ir_specs.statement: If no options are given, uname acts as if the -s option were given.
ir_specs.source_section: 21.4 uname: Print system information
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: Without options, uname behaves as if -s were given.

question_specs.question: The `pwd` command prints the name of the current directory.
question_specs.explanation: `pwd` prints the name of the current directory.

question_specs.question: The `basename` command prints the directory part of a path.
question_specs.explanation: `basename` removes leading directory components rather than printing them.

question_specs.question: If a string contains no slash, `dirname` prints `.`.
question_specs.explanation: For input with no slash, `dirname` prints `.` to mean the current directory.

question_specs.question: The `true` command is used as a placeholder where an unsuccessful command is needed.
question_specs.explanation: `true` is used where a successful command is needed because it returns exit status 0.

question_specs.question: The `false` command returns exit status 1, meaning failure.
question_specs.explanation: `false` does nothing except return exit status 1, which indicates failure.

question_specs.question: The `dirname` command prints only the final slash-delimited component of a path.
question_specs.explanation: `dirname` prints everything except the final slash-delimited component.

question_specs.question: With `pwd --logical`, the command always resolves every symbolic link in the printed path.
question_specs.explanation: `pwd --logical` can use the `PWD` environment value instead of fully resolving symbolic links.

question_specs.question: With `pwd --physical`, the printed path can still contain symbolic links.
question_specs.explanation: `pwd --physical` prints a fully resolved path whose components are actual directory names, not symbolic links.

question_specs.question: If `basename` is given a suffix that matches the end of the name, it can remove that suffix.
question_specs.explanation: A suffix identical to the end of the name is removed by `basename`.

question_specs.question: The `basename --zero` option ends each line with a newline character.
question_specs.explanation: `basename --zero` uses an ASCII NUL byte instead of a newline.

question_specs.question: The `echo` command writes strings separated by spaces and adds a newline after the last one.
question_specs.explanation: `echo` writes each string to standard output with spaces between strings and a trailing newline by default.

question_specs.question: The `echo -n` option forces a trailing newline to be printed.
question_specs.explanation: `echo -n` suppresses the trailing newline.

question_specs.question: The `echo -e` option enables interpretation of backslash escapes.
question_specs.explanation: `echo -e` enables interpretation of backslash-escaped characters.

question_specs.question: The `echo -E` option enables interpretation of backslash escapes.
question_specs.explanation: `echo -E` disables interpretation of backslash escapes.

question_specs.question: The `printf` command does formatted printing of text.
question_specs.explanation: `printf` is used for formatted printing of text.

question_specs.question: The `printf` command ignores both `%` directives and backslash escapes in its format string.
question_specs.explanation: `printf` interprets `%` directives and backslash escapes in the format string.

question_specs.question: The `yes` command repeats its command line arguments, separated by spaces and followed by a newline, until it is killed.
question_specs.explanation: `yes` keeps printing its arguments until it is interrupted or killed.

question_specs.question: If `yes` is run with no arguments, it repeatedly prints `n`.
question_specs.explanation: With no arguments, `yes` repeatedly prints `y` followed by a newline.

question_specs.question: The `printenv` command prints environment variable values.
question_specs.explanation: `printenv` prints environment variable values.

question_specs.question: If no variable is specified, `printenv` prints only one environment variable.
question_specs.explanation: When no variable is specified, `printenv` prints the value of every environment variable.

question_specs.question: The `printenv --null` option ends each line with a NUL byte instead of a newline.
question_specs.explanation: `printenv --null` uses an ASCII NUL byte terminator instead of a newline.

question_specs.question: The `tty` command prints the file name of the terminal connected to standard output.
question_specs.explanation: `tty` checks the terminal connected to standard input, not standard output.

question_specs.question: If standard input is not a terminal, `tty` prints `not a tty`.
question_specs.explanation: The manual says `tty` prints `not a tty` when standard input is not a terminal.

question_specs.question: The `logname` command prints the calling user's name from a system-maintained file.
question_specs.explanation: `logname` prints the calling user's name as found in a system-maintained file.

question_specs.question: If there is no entry for the calling process, `logname` exits with status 0.
question_specs.explanation: If there is no entry for the calling process, `logname` exits with status 1.

question_specs.question: The `whoami` command prints the user name associated with the current effective user ID.
question_specs.explanation: `whoami` prints the user name associated with the current effective user ID.

question_specs.question: The `whoami` command is equivalent to `id -gn`.
question_specs.explanation: The manual says `whoami` is equivalent to `id -un`, not `id -gn`.

question_specs.question: The `date` command displays the date and time.
question_specs.explanation: `date` displays the date and time.

question_specs.question: With `--set`, the `date` command sets the date and time and then exits without displaying it.
question_specs.explanation: With `--set`, `date` sets the date and time before displaying it.

question_specs.question: If no options are given, `uname` acts as if `-s` were given.
question_specs.explanation: Without options, `uname` behaves as if the `-s` option were given.
